title: Slip trailing on air-dry clay
subtitle: Applying raised lines of slip from a squeeze bottle
excerpt: Slip trailing is a decoration technique in which fluid clay slip is squeezed through a small nozzle in continuous lines onto the clay surface, building up a raised pattern. On air-dry clay, the trailed slip dries with the piece and is sealed as part of the finished surface. A plastic squeeze bottle with a fine nozzle is the standard tool. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Slip trailing technique. Standard English slipware decoration. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: is the technique behind traditional English slipware plates and dishes: the dotted and swirled patterns in contrasting
that characterise the style are all applied by trailing. The technique requires no drawing skill, it rewards confident, fluid movement over precision. On air-dry clay, the slip bonds to the
surface and becomes part of the piece once dry.

attrs.body: The slip should flow freely when the bottle is tilted but hold a raised line when applied. Test on paper: the trailed line should stay rounded, not spread flat like water. If it spreads, the slip is too thin, stir in a small amount of dry clay powder. If it strings and breaks, it is too thick, add water a teaspoon at a time.
attrs.title: Trailing slip consistency

items.name: Coloured clay slip or acrylic paint slip
Slip trailer (rubber bulb or plastic squeeze bottle)

content.text: Step 1: Fill the trailer and practise

Fill the slip trailer about two-thirds full with trailing slip. Practise on a piece of paper before working on the clay: trail straight lines, curves, dots, and spirals. The trailer should be held like a pen, nozzle down, with the tip 3 to 5 mm above the surface. Consistent hand speed and consistent squeeze pressure produce even lines.
Step 2: Apply the design to the clay

Work on a level clay surface at leather-hard. Move the trailer in a single continuous stroke for each line. Lift the nozzle cleanly at the end of a line to prevent a blob forming. For a dot, hold the trailer still and squeeze once, then lift. Do not stop mid-line, a hesitation shows as a thicker section.

Feathering is a classic slipware technique: after applying parallel lines of contrasting slip, draw a pin or needle tool across the lines at 90 degrees before the slip sets. The tool picks up the top slip and drags it through the bottom colour, creating a herringbone or feather pattern. Work quickly, the window before the lines set is short.
Leave the trailed design to dry undisturbed. The slip will dry to a slightly raised texture that is visible after sealing. Allow to reach bone-dry, then seal with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1.

items.fix: Thicken the slip with a small amount of dry clay powder. Ensure the clay surface is at leather-hard before trailing.
items.cause: Slip too thin or clay surface still wet.
items.symptom: Trailed lines spread flat and lose the raised profile

items.fix: Thin the slip slightly with water and practise maintaining a consistent slow speed. The nozzle tip should be just above the surface so the slip touches it and stays continuous.
items.cause: Slip too thick, or trailer moved too fast.
items.symptom: Line breaks and strings as the trailer is moved
